trajectory_summary: The call books cleanly and Benjamin is cooperative throughout, but the energy is driven almost entirely by Benjamin's own motivation — Meg follows the checklist without engaging the unusual lead profile. The comparison-shopping frame recurs three times and is never addressed; the grid-reliability motivation is surfaced and dropped; the partnership/sponsorship idea is ignored entirely. The appointment is real but fragile: Benjamin is still in active comparison mode and expects information to be sent before the call.
outcome_dispute: The appointment is booked and Benjamin confirms it with his calendar in hand — the booking outcome is valid. However, the call's energy shape is stagnant rather than built: Meg did not convert Benjamin's high initial engagement into emotional commitment to Ion specifically. He is booked but not differentiated from the other companies he is comparing.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I just really wanna get everything on solar just in case being in the mountains, I know there's been times when they've had electricity cut out.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Benjamin hands Meg his primary motivation unprompted and she does not probe it once. This is the verify item with the highest coaching leverage: the emotional anchor question is where the closer's entry point gets built. On this call, the closer walks in without knowing that outage protection — not cost savings — is what Benjamin actually cares about. The Powerwall cross-sell, the appointment framing, and the text tieback all depend on this anchor being captured. Meg's pattern is to check the qualification boxes and move to scheduling; the depth question is the gap.

key_moments.quote: Alright. Yeah. You can give them you can give them my email, send me all the information in the packet, and then, you know, I don't wanna have anybody come out before the decision's made
key_moments.stewart_read: Benjamin is not requesting the formal solar design proposal the schema hard-rule addresses — he is asking for general company information to include in his comparison. This is the 'already_saw_design' comparison-shopping pattern, not the 'send_proposal_before_meeting' hard rule. Meg sidesteps the request by pivoting to qualification, which works procedurally but leaves the comparison-shopping frame unaddressed. The closer will inherit a customer who still expects a packet. Strategic read: Meg may have deliberately deferred to avoid a confrontation that could derail the call. Reactive read: she missed the comparison_framing rebuttal entirely. The reactive read is more supported — there is no evidence of a deliberate setup for a later move.

key_moments.quote: sometimes these programs, you know, if the cause is adequate to meet your your your company, some of these things we're looking to have maybe installed by the people, have the people learn, add just being cooked up, and then maybe the promotion can ripple out.
key_moments.stewart_read: Benjamin is floating a promotional partnership concept — Ion installs at his educational center as a learning/demo project in exchange for community exposure. This is not a standard residential purchase frame. Meg does not acknowledge it at any point in the call. This is not a disqualifier, but it is a signal about how Benjamin is thinking about the transaction — and the closer needs to know about it. The verify.interest_in_solar item asks 'what interested you in solar?' — the depth question that should have surfaced this. Meg never asks it.

key_moments.quote: I just really wanna get everything on solar just in case being in the mountains, I know there's been times when they've had electricity cut out.
key_moments.stewart_read: Benjamin explicitly names grid reliability and outage protection as his primary motivation. This is a direct Powerwall signal — battery backup is exactly what addresses this concern, and Tesla Powerwall is the only battery brand Ion carries. Meg does not probe it, does not anchor it, and does not reference it again. The closer will walk in without knowing this is the emotional core of Benjamin's interest.

key_moments.quote: K. Perfect. Now when I'm looking on Google Maps, I am seeing there's some trees going on. Are those
key_moments.stewart_read: Meg proactively checks Google Maps and raises the shading concern before Benjamin does — this is good instinct. Benjamin's response reveals a three-allotment property with a clear southern exposure plan and a garage on the northern side. The property is more complex than a standard residential install, and Benjamin has already thought through placement. Meg correctly identifies the ground-mount limitation and redirects to the northern building. This is one of the stronger moments in the call — Meg uses available information to qualify the install site rather than waiting for the customer to describe it.
